preamble: The transformation from the voiceless retroflex affricate /ʈʂ/ to the voiced postalveolar affricate /dʒ/ is a phonetic shift involving both a change in phonation and a modification of the place of articulation. This sound change is characterized by the vocal cords beginning to vibrate during the articulation (voicing), and the tongue tip moving slightly forward from a retroflex position to a postalveolar one (de-retroflexion or fronting). This particular shift is observed as a common, though non-standard, realization in certain linguistic contexts.

examples.note: Retroflexes (e.g., Pinyin 'zh-') are realized as postalveolar consonants.
